So I really didn't know what to throw in on the 'excite' stage, to be honest, but just take it through it. The way we get a lot of people to come into our Can you tell us what you do first? Sorry. Yeah, sorry. I have a real estate company. A real estate company in Canada. Um and uh yeah this is the way we generate leads essentially. So first thing we do is we throw up a Google search ad for, you know, wealth MLS, homes for sale, et cetera, keyword searches. Um, no remarketing currently in place, so it's kind of doing that— driving them to a relevant property search page. So it's literally a page of properties. That's the valuable content we're giving them: properties. And then one or two things can happen.
Either people call us directly from that. from that line, or they create an account. The annoying thing is this isn't like a typical like squeeze page where only one thing can happen. There's multiple things that people can do to create an account. They can save favorites, create a search, just sign up for an account for no reason. And those are essentially the typical outcomes, which I've documented here. And requesting more information for a property or schedule is showing. Those are essentially the four signup methods that it can make to create an account. And then when they create an account, they essentially go into a sales follow-up call and email sequence. All right. Sorry, a safe search. Yeah. And then if they save a search, they'll also get every day, if a new property matches that criteria, they'll be sent.
That property as well. Right. So. So a safe search will have two things happen and all the other ones will just have. The sales team engaged into a follow-up sequence as well as some emails that are going out right now. Um, if we have a call with them, so if we connect. A couple of things could happen, and I didn't really know how to illustrate this well. So we re-engaged them with a three, six, or 12-month out nurture campaign, which I have it orange. It needs a lot of work. Otherwise, the other outcomes are a race to the face. 75% of people that see the first agent do business with them. Like, hey, I want to go see this property. You want to come in the office and qualify?
No, I just want to see this property. Okay. Race to the face. Then try to deliver the aha to get them to book a discovery call or a consultation. Discovery Call is essentially a 15-minute, 20-minute consult to get to know them on their goals. And if we can convince them to do our full consultation, this is where we try to sign them up as a client. Our typical deal value is about $25,000 to $30,000 per deal. So it's a bit of a process. And then, essentially, I've. Um... And popped it in. So like. If we race to the face, then we meet the prospect. And then these are the possible outcomes. We book a discovery call. Um. Which then, if they show up, you know, then we want to book the strategy.
Sorry, we want to deliver the strategy call and then book the consultation. and if um, and if we race the face, and we just book the consultation, we'll just skip all this and just go directly to the consult. um, yeah. and then essentially the way we send them is, do they show up to the appointment? deliver the aha, deliver the consultation. and then either we sign them up, either they're ready to go. and if they're not, we try to. you know, put them back into the nurture campaign. um, and, uh, if they're like, hey, we're not really sure, you know, we'll do a free trial, quote unquote, where it's like. hey, let's go look at a couple of properties, make sure the relationship's good, make sure we're adding some value to you.
And then obviously try to sign them later to have an exclusive representation agreement with them. Um, or they do sign up and the way we upsell is we charge a transaction fee, setup fee. For listings, it's $1,500 up front. And for a buyer, it's $495. And that's why we get a little bump on our profitability. And then we start the fulfillment engine. So I guess the questions I have is: I want to start layering in some. I'm not tracking any of these. I'm tracking some of these metrics. My marketing metrics are not tracked well, and I'm not really sure how to. Take more advantage of the traffic we're getting because I would really prefer to skip all that, all those steps that I just went through. Yeah.
Go directly to a strategy call and build copy and remarketing advertisements that convince the people to, hey, I need to book a 15-minute call with these people. So we're now skipping all the follow-up, all the re-engagement emails, and we're just getting right to the strategy call. Right out of the hop— so like, just like a lot of the online marketers do, the direct response marketing, right? You're booking a call first and then you're getting a value delivered and so on and so forth. Tchau. The problem with marketing these days is that it's created too much friction for sales. I'm saying that because... I'm a salesperson myself. Although my superpower is marketing, I started in sales before I started marketing. So I'm giving you permission to just have people talk to you, because that's actually...
not an issue that's not a problem. I know the internet marketers of today are like pumping out a million like messaging and generating millions and all that great stuff. But in legacy industries like real estate, it's the human to human connection that's going to win. And so, the more friction that you create between the human connection, you're in a unique industry where the more friction you create between the human connection. It'd be different if you're doing real estate investing, where you're talking to investors, where there's things that you want to talk about. When I go onto a car lot, I used to sell cars. I used to be in the car business. If I called or wanted to know about a Corolla, a Toyota Corolla, let's look for Toyota.
And I had to go through a million steps just to talk to somebody, just to take a test drive and do all these things. I'm probably going to go to the next person. So, if you don't have metrics on this, that this works, and I'm not saying to throw it away in any way, shape or form. I would like my advice is going to be that, um. My advice is going to be that also look at your organic trends. See who's just coming to your site and just going to those pages and looking for property and see if just picking up the phone and calling them or just giving them a number that says 'Call this right now' and talk to somebody. See how that converts, because if you don't need to do all of that.
You know, because like when you have Google traffic, it's high intent. And so. I don't know how long it's going to take to get somebody on a call. Necessarily with that process. And then I don't mean like... How long it takes to convince them to get on the call, because your process is convincing them to get on the call. A lot of them might be ready. So, um, I guess I think you're showing me your website, which I see the phone number at the top. And I see the book of strategy fall. I started to call in my isn't—I don't from like I work with a lot of real estate folks and It's kind of like, I'm going to explain this first before I... explain the methodology, but um.
There's a girl here that decided to open a book bar. It was basically a place where you could go read books, do work, listen to some live music, order some wine or beer, and have little like hand finger foods and stuff. And it was a really cool location. It was going to be open from like 11 a. m. to 2 a. m. It wasn't like a club. It was just like a place where you go and work late and not have to be stuck at home. This is like six years ago, before the pandemic. And what was weird, it was great, but what was weird about it is that when you went in there, they had menus, and if you had your menu sideways, that means you wanted service.
If you had your menu upwards, it means you didn't. They were getting a lot of complaints on Yelp because that's not how people are used to doing business. That's not how people are used to. When it comes to service, do you see people coming to your table, checking on you? Et cetera, et cetera. And for people to try to remember, like changing your menu a certain way. And then people didn't actually come to your prior table when you had a certain way. So I say all that to say, like. You're getting traffic. And so I wouldn't create barriers to get to what they want. Um And I see that you have shown me your website here. So if you wanted to maybe. I don't want to interrupt you in case you had something you wanted to add.
No, I appreciate the feedback. And I mean, that's how we're doing it right now. It's like, so we don't do any forced registration either, where the old school way of real estate marketing was. Do the same search ad or Facebook ads and then people come to your website and be like, 'Immediately gate the gate the listings and say, 'Give me your information.' So we got a lot of really low quality stuff and then I ran Facebook and Google side by side. Shut off forced registration and Google just absolutely crushed Facebook for us right. So it was super, yeah, it was super high quality. You know, people that gave us their information actually wanted us to talk to them. We got no fake, like we have a 98% connection rate.
So we are definitely very open for very fluid conversations. So I mean, every single page will have a contact us phone number at the top of every page. Plus, with follow-up boss, if they are assigned to an agent, they will have a call and text me button right there. I set up a fake person called Sam for Samantha or Sam. So you can hire an ISA for both. Female and male versions of that person that automatically send out the emails. So it's like a sales executive assistant, so you're not assigning leads to an agent that might not be able to handle it. Um, so like all of my, all my systems are pretty dialed in. In terms of that, I mean, our speed of response average is usually about 10 minutes.
We try to get on the phone in, like, the first minute while they're still on the website, obviously. So, yeah, we're not forcing anyone. We've got about 20,000 people a month at this site, 15,000. Yeah, about 10,000, that's organic. And then, if we really want to drive a lot of high quality traffic, I'm spending about 350 a day on Google Ads. But, like, I mean, The other companies are trying to get me to do, you know, prospecting ads on Google Display Network and stuff like that. But I really feel like that's a better remarketing. Thing and I'd rather I'd rather remarket them to a strategy call because they've already been here, they already know. They could search properties on my website. So like, I wasn't thinking about leading with strategy calls.
Like, hey, you know, how can I write my email content and my follow-up ads to like... Give them some nuggets to be like. You know, what you really need is a plan, and let's get on the phone together and make this plan for you. Right. Because that's just a way for me to explain. I don't know. Because we call these people and we and we set up discovery calls with them as our first step. So, like, all this stuff happens that I showed you in my value map. It all this stuff is happening. It's just that, um, the remarketing isn't on. My email sequences are garbage. And like, the only thing that's really working well is the traffic. And then, and then, the follow-up calls.
The really big thing I find that I struggle with is that not having a forced registration or having any kind of value to add, except for these properties. I'm running a bit of a 1% conversion rate on traffic. Right. So that's super low. So, from a user experience, your biggest opportunity? Because it sounds like everything's going right and you just have a UX issue. You basically just need to, like, your strategy call isn't really a strategy call. It's more like, 'Why do people want to talk to agents?' That's a customer experience question. It even knows where I'm going with this. From a customer experience standpoint, right? So what that means is: Why would someone want to talk to an agent if there's all this information here? And the answer to that is.
I'm sick of searching high and low for a million houses. Can someone help me find more houses like this in my budget? Right. Right. So they don't want a strategy call. They want to be triggered by the thing that they want. Looking for more houses like this. Give us a call, we can help you. Yes, it's a strategy call that's what you're giving them. But it's showing them what they want and then giving them what they need. Um... Ultimately, right? Because if you're searching for a strategy call, you don't know where they're at in their customer journey. Right. You don't know where they're at in their decision process yet. Right. You don't necessarily know, like people could be looking at houses for interior decorating ideas.
People could be looking for houses because their neighbors sold theirs and they're maybe thinking about selling theirs or who knows. There's a lot of different reasons why people go looking for homes. And so, from a customer experience standpoint, it's like, okay. What are the reasons why someone will or would want to talk to a realtor? That aren't my reasons, but are their reasons. And so when you just change your little CTAs on your buttons or your little lead-ins, that... Please. It takes the intent and it matches it with what they're wanting. Tchau. Um, just those little tweaks could have a huge impact on people. Getting that conversion from one percent to maybe just a little bit higher. Even if it was 2%, that'd be great. I mean, yeah. Mm-hmm.
Yeah. Like, I mean, um, Yeah. I just, I would like more, more of the traffic to, you know, request information. Cause, like we have an average, the bounce rate's pretty low around like 43% bounce. And time on site is usually around three minutes and 25 seconds. That's an average customer spending on our site. So we have a huge amount of engagement. So I think people are getting what they want out of here. It's just we're not giving them that next nugget of like. Oh, hey, you know like, like, you made a great point, you know, why would they want to talk to us like, why book a strategy call? And I'm not sure if I should experiment with pop-ups or like headers, some kind of thing, or like you said, change the language of the buttons.
But now we're getting into it. But yeah. User experience is very direct. And customer experience is very direct, right? So user experience is what they do on the page and making sure that you're directing them to the next obvious step. So when you say 'more info,' like contact an agent for more info. It's like. Maybe you've had a little summary up there. Like we have a lot more information on this property, like history, XYZ, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah. Happy to send that to you. Give us your info and we'll go ahead and make sure that you have all the info if you're interested in this property. Scheduling a visit is super direct because you know anyone that follows that out wants to actually you know, look at that property.
So that's actually pretty direct. Um, what are some other logical next steps as to reasons why people would want to contact you guys? They need to be on this page. If you want to do this, do that. Like you got to be very, very specific. Okay. And very direct. Maybe they don't want to fill that out. Maybe they can just call like, 'Hey, um, you can also just give us a call for more info on this property too.' Um, we can, you know, make sure that we get that sent to you right away. No problem. Um, Maybe they're in the neighborhood and want to just check it out or something. Who knows? Um, what they don't want to be is sold to or be harassed when they're maybe not ready yet.
So you got to sometimes give people permission to do their research how they want to do their research. And so I think your approach needs to be to study your customer more and to study what they're wanting with CX— customer experience, so you can do some research on customer experience. What are they wanting? Are they finding the thing that they're looking for? I like doing little pop-ups, like not little pop-ups, but like either use a hotjar or some sort of chat bot that says, 'Hey, did you find what you're looking for?' People fill that out and they'll say, 'No, I was looking for blank.' And so now that'll help you understand what user experience your customers are looking for, like how to adjust your user experience so the customers can find it, right?
Um, and then are there aspects of your of your user experience that are missing that are part of a customer's research that they really either want to see or before they talk to an agent? So I think you're almost there. I think at this point, your tweaks are UX and CX, and not necessarily value engine tweaks if that's already in place. And now, once you start to get some of that data, you can start to readjust your messaging. For your follow-up emails, follow-up texts, follow-up calls, so they're not so intrusive, so they're not so... off base as far as the customer experience goes. So, like, triggering like a pop-up messenger to be like, 'Hey, just curious to see if you got everything you're looking for and just seeing what the what the answers come back as— so and just like building the follow-up.
Based on that, right? Because we know when we have a strategy call with someone, there's four steps we essentially go through. A, we figure out what they're actually looking for, so their goals and their timing. Actually, help reverse engineer their timing to actually show them how long it could take and people really are like, 'Oh, I actually didn't know it takes that long because like closing times and all that processing time. So, A, they had no idea that they needed to start two weeks ago. And then, three, setting up a financial game plan because a lot of people don't know all the components that are set up in the cost of acquisition, acquiring, and disposing your real estate.
So, when we, when we actually have someone through a consultation, they're like, 'Oh my God, I didn't know all this stuff was happening.' So thankful. I jumped on that. So we know. When we get them there, they have so much value. And it is like that— aha, like, wow— these guys are incredible. I'm so well prepared now. How do I, how do I give her that? How do I give that to them quicker? But that's, but that's like, yeah, that's again, UX and CX, right? Like. You're saying strategy call. That's not clear to me what that is because like, so on this page, I would add like, you can afford this home. Most people don't believe that they can afford a home like this, but we've helped thousands of people get into homes.
That they didn't think they could afford. We'd love to set up a financial game plan for you. Give us a call. Let's talk strategy. Boom, here we go. Like there's no obligation. There's nothing. We're just— we're just here to help people get into their dream homes. Let's go. That's like a really great story that you can put in your sidebar. Um, and I would maybe play with the order of it. Yeah. So that's. So that's. You know, because, again, it is about customer experience, not how you want your customers to interact with you. It's how do you fit into their process. Right. I'm down the road from you in the big in the big city in Toronto, so we're looking at this, because of the work that we do, because it's digital, we can do it anywhere in North America. But we've been focusing mostly locally, pretty locally. But our goal to meet our growth goals, we're going to have to push beyond that. and go much further, not only just across Canada, but in North America wide.
Our target is business to business. services companies, mostly tech services. So we're going out to all the MSPs, IT services companies out there. According to our stats, there's about 525,000 of them, which is kind of insane. And over 50,000 of them are in Canada. So these are the— from the really small ones, there's people who are going around doing break and fix, you know, for your computers. All the way up to like those really large organizations that handle all your help desk and all that kind of stuff. So those are our clients. I've been working with them for a long time. They need lots of help. They're engineers. They're definitely not marketers. We find that webinars are okay as a way to reach them, but we can't reach all of them that way because they're so busy during the day.
And so we're also looking for other ways to reach that target market demographic. We used to be able to do it in person. Because they would sort of take off and, you know, go to events and things like that. Since we haven't been able to do that for the last couple of years, we're still searching for that right. You know, right fix, you know what I mean? So webinars for sure. And we do work on LinkedIn. Absolutely. So we do those two things, but I don't know if there's anything else you could add or any insights. Um, I. T. industry, so you know, whenever I do an audit for a client, um, to try to figure out where the market is. Like I was kind of saying for Steve, like no matter who we work with, we do an audit.
Some industries I know better than others, and some industries are a little bit more. I don't want to say 'interruptive,' but like 'disruptive.' So nobody knows anything about the industry the way the client does. So we're both at a disadvantage. So we have to do the studies anyway. So I would do an audit and I've talked about this a lot on different calls, but we do a full UX and CX audit. And what that allows us to do is it allows us to create really in-depth buyer personas. Like, who are these people that you work with? Is it the actual technicians that you're talking to? Is it the actual dispatch people that you're talking to? Or is it the actual people who are salespeople that...
work with the dispatch people, work out with the technicians or whoever, like who in the company makes these decisions on who does their marketing. What is the thing that's in their head that triggers them to think, 'I need someone to help with my marketing?' Is it the days that they have slow days? Is it the days that they... uh, are just doing tinkering work and they would rather do like larger contract work. What is that thing that they say when you talk to them that makes them realize they want help or that they need help with something? And what is that thing that they think they need help with? Yeah. Well, the, you know, very important questions. And the, it depends on the size, of course, of the business, right?
So the size that we're going out to are people who've been in business for a while, they're doing over half a million dollars worth of business. So they've, you know, They've been out there for a little bit. And they have some organization. They don't typically have dedicated sales and marketing internally. So they've done all their work through word of mouth or just whatever. They've grown to that spot. And what triggers for them is that they want to either one of two things. They want to sell their business. So they need to get to a certain revenue level in order to be attractive to buyers. There's been a bit of a consolidation in the industry where bigger players are looking to consolidate high-value, smaller players. So there's been quite a lot of buyouts.
That's what I've been observing. That's what we read in the industry. But to be able to be bought out, which is what everybody wants, really, they have to be at a certain level of growth and maturity to be able to do that. So that's one thing. And the second is so that they can actually hire. Some internal full-time staff will take over. The sales and marketing function so that the owners or the— you know, the prime, the directors, whoever it is— don't have to do it. Generally speaking, they don't like doing it; they're these the people who go into this business, you know. They love working in tech. That's what they do, right? That makes sense. Yeah, I have to. Yeah. Clients in like the commercial security space and the commercial cleaning space.
Same kind of thing. They don't have internal marketing. But they do it. They run ads. They do all the things, right? Um, so I'll rephrase it. So there's a study and I know I've talked about this a lot. So he was probably sick of hearing it. You know, I'm going to go with the moment of truth. The moment of truth is like when I'm making a recipe for cookies and I realize I have no sugar. That's my trigger. Like I realized I have no sugar, so I have options. I can either call Postmates and have them deliver the sugar for 10 bucks because I'm too lazy to go to the store. I can get in my car and go to the store.
I can go knock on my neighbor's door and figure out if they have sugar and let me borrow it. I have options, right? And so it's really understanding what is that trigger that makes them, is this like... So, yes, they want to sell their business. Right. But what is that? What is that one trigger? It's like we need more contracts. Yeah, yeah that definitely would be, or we need better contracts or we want bigger contracts. Like, if they're sick and tired of getting all these little guys and all their competitors are getting big guys, that's a fantastic campaign. It's a fantastic webinar. It's fantastic. Whatever to go pitch these IT companies with, just saying, 'Hey, here's how we're helping,' you know.
Small IT companies get $100,000 projects, a million dollar projects, which is what they want, right? And if you have a proven way that you can do this, they don't care how you do it. They just care that you can. Right. And so, just building a collection of those triggers. Um, of what they're looking for and what they want, or just acknowledging where they're at in their process to where they're thinking, 'Yes, I need a marketing person' and/ or they can be convinced, like, they might have a problem that being caused because they don't have marketing. They have. Maybe their technicians are spread too thin because they can't afford to hire someone because their sales aren't consistent. Right. Their stuff's just way too inconsistent. They can solve that by getting better contracts.
Maybe they're just. Their contracts aren't structured well. So that's going to give you a way to structure your content and your outreach so that they see you as an expert and really help them understand that you know their business so intimately. You are the expert for them, so that when they go to sign up for these webinars, there's more than just this webinar. They're like, 'Wow, your brand is really like in tune with how we as IT folk.' Think and what we need. Um, so you get that. That buy-in. Because again, you don't like. You said they don't have time. They're busy. Why should they do this? Like, Getting into the customer's head and doing that customer experience research. Will really help, and and you know, doing some real good customer journey mapping as well.
Yeah, absolutely. I've worked in the industry for quite a while, but I also think that there's always room to do more. Right? They always, especially as things are shifting, COVID shifted everything for everybody. IT was really busy, super busy over COVID. They were probably more busy than ever before. Because of all the changes. And so they haven't actually had a chance to stop in the last two years. And think, because they've been running off their feet. But now they are starting to, and maybe that's what we need to be talking about. No, I think your growth engine's good, but what comes with CX...
The cons of CX research is that now you're also looking at their, not just like what they're searching or like what topics you're talking about, but like. Where do they like? How do they make that decision? Like, how do they go from like, okay, I need more contracts. What do they do next? Do they search on Google? Do they search, do they go into specific like groups that are specific to it companies? Do they go to certain events, trade shows, to seek these answers? Are they looking for. Um, a guide or like articles say how am I, how do I get these things? For example, you just talked about remote work— one of the biggest challenges of remote work is it, and security.
Because now the states are mandating that you know, companies buy laptops for their employees and then they have to have VPNs and IPS and it's like, Sure, they used to do that in some form or fashion, but having to set up home workstations for people and create processes. They're busy. It's like, okay, well now how do you structure that to make the most money? And so on and so forth. Like they're going to be searching for that information online, Google, whatever. So it's like, are you there when they need that info and they can switch them over to why that's a marketing and sales function and that you can support them and structuring their offering, structuring their visibility so that. Because I'm sure the competitors don't have time or haven't had time to update their websites.
and their materials to include this. It's like okay. Well, you really can't wait. Let's go right. Like you, you need to take advantage of this opportunity because companies are literally their.
